title: Help with quotes please
wikitext:
[]: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by Shin-seiki » Sun Apr 15, 2007 1:10 am I have a couple Eva-related quotes rattling around in my brain, but I can't for the life of me remember where to find them. One I believe was by Kazuya Tsurumaki, who, when asked by Eva fans at a con about whose soul was in Unit-00, replied something like "I think it was a clone of Rei". This, of course, is very interesting, in that he refers here to Rei, and NOT RITSUKO'S MOM. Anyway, if I had the exact quote available, I figure I could lay that on the next bozo that brings up the Naoko heresy. The other one was concerned Yoshiyuki Tomino, of Gundam and Ideon fame; I recall reading once that he, upon being told that Hideaki Anno claimed that he was inspired by Tomino's work, retorted something like "Ugh! Don't go blaming that show (NGE) on me!" Any help nailing either one of these down would be appreciated.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Apr 15, 2007 1:19 am Don't have a clue about either, but as far as the latter goes: I can't imagine any professional saying something like that. Even if they really disliked a work, if they knew the creator was inspired by them, I can't imagine them saying something like that. It would just seem really, really mean and pointless.

[]:             Quote Post by Shin-seiki » Sun Apr 15, 2007 1:28 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Don't have a clue about either, but as far as the latter goes: I can't imagine any professional saying something like that. Even if they really disliked a work, if they knew the creator was inspired by them, I can't imagine them saying something like that. It would just seem really, really mean and pointless.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Apr 15, 2007 1:33 am Shin-seiki wrote: I think the point was that Tomino was really jealous and resentful of Anno's success with Evangelion. Supposedly 'Brain Powered' was intended as his answer to NGE, but it languishes in relative obscurity, while NGE is ever-so-much more famous and popular.

[]: Re: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by Ornette » Sun Apr 15, 2007 2:21 am Shin-seiki wrote: One I believe was by Kazuya Tsurumaki, who, when asked by Eva fans at a con about whose soul was in Unit-00, replied something like "I think it was a clone of Rei". This, of course, is very interesting, in that he refers here to Rei, and NOT RITSUKO'S MOM. Anyway, if I had the exact quote available, I figure I could lay that on the next bozo that brings up the Naoko heresy.

[]: Re: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by Shin-seiki » Sun Apr 15, 2007 7:55 am Ornette wrote: WOW. I'm surprised that quote isn't plastered all over the internet

[]: Re: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Sun Apr 15, 2007 8:13 am Shin-seiki wrote: Anyway, if I had the exact quote available, I figure I could lay that on the next bozo that brings up the Naoko heresy.

[]: Re: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by Executor » Sun Apr 15, 2007 9:57 am ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: Shin-seiki wrote: Anyway, if I had the exact quote available, I figure I could lay that on the next bozo that brings up the Naoko heresy.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Apr 15, 2007 8:15 pm The only thing I know about Yoshiyuki Tomino is in the copy of my first Animerica issue, the Sailormoon movies issue (Ahh, nostalgia!), has the following: Answering a question about how cinematic elements is the responsibility of the director/creator (not the whole quote, only the part about Eva): Animerica , Vol 8 #2 (March 2000) wrote: Tomino: For instance, Brain Powered came out after Evangelion did, so I am often asked questions similar to yours about the connection between them, but in reality the plans for Brain Powered and the overall story had all been completed before Evangelion came out. I never meant Brain Powered to be an antithesis to Evangelion. I knew when I saw Evangelion that Brain Powered would be called an antithesis to it, but I didn't want to change my plans any, so I just resigned myself to that. This is connected with my wish for more animators to see themselves as entertainers. I don't think I succeeded with Brain Powered, and I don't think it was very good with entertainment--but there was one thing I did try to do with it. If 100 people come to see an anime with giant robots, then chances are are that not every one of those 100 people will be a huge fan of robot anime. What I wanted to do was to make an anime that had a truly interesting story that wouldn't cause the people who watched it to have a nervous breakdown. I also tried to make a story that would tell anime fans that there were often other things out there better than anime. That's the goal I challenged myself to do. I don't think the series itself was a success, though, I have to admit that. So I was very upset when I saw Evangelion, because it was apparent to me that the people who made it weren't thinking at all about making fun for or gaining the sympathy of the audience. Instead they tried to convince the audience to admit that everybody is sick, practically in the middle of a nervous breakdown, all the time. I don't think you should show things like that to everybody. It's not entertainment for the masses--it's much more interested in admitting that we're all depressed nervous wrecks, I thought. It was a work that told people it was okay to be depressed, and it accepted the psychological state that said if you don't like the way the world works, then it's okay to just pick up a gun and attack someone. I don't think that's a real work of art. When people see that, they begin to realize they are the same way. I think that we should try to show people how to live healthier, fuller lives, to foster their identity as a part of their community, and to encourage them to work happily until they die. I can't accept any work that doesn't say that. Animerica: Is that different from your downbeat endings? Tomino: I make sure my audience knows it's fiction and that what happens to my characters doesn't necessarily say anything about their own lives.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Apr 15, 2007 9:37 pm Wow, that quote is beyond ignorant. It's damn near bordering on retarded. I lost whatever respect I might've had for Tomino - not because of his dislike of NGE, but because of his utter ignorance of what true art is supposed to be. Animerica , Vol 8 #2 (March 2000) wrote: This is connected with my wish for more animators to see themselves as entertainers.

[]:             Quote Post by Katayoku no Tenshi » Mon Apr 16, 2007 9:16 am ^ :shock: wow OWN'D Sailor Star Dust wrote: I admit that Eva CAN make you think that way about the world ("we're all nervous wrecks", "it's okay to attack someone" I'm assuming that's a reference to Shin-chan and his giving humanity the finger during Third Impact Pre-Instrumentality??), but it only affects the impressionable types/those who already have problems like depression. *coughs*

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Jun 11, 2008 2:47 pm BUMPED because I've been looking for this thread for some time now.

[]: Re: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by mrpanda » Wed Jun 11, 2008 3:42 pm Shin-seiki wrote: This, of course, is very interesting, in that he refers here to Rei, and NOT RITSUKO'S MOM. Anyway, if I had the exact quote available, I figure I could lay that on the next bozo that brings up the Naoko heresy.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Wed Jun 11, 2008 3:43 pm It wont once everyone gets started again...

[]: Re: Help with quotes, please             Quote Post by TemporalKnight » Wed Jun 11, 2008 4:05 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: mrpanda wrote: First time ive heard the naoko theory and tbh it makes more sense to me than the Rei clone theory.

[]:             Quote Post by honsou » Wed Jun 11, 2008 11:12 pm Animerica , Vol 8 #2 (March 2000) wrote: Tomino: For instance, Brain Powered came out after Evangelion did, so I am often asked questions similar to yours about the connection between them, but in reality the plans for Brain Powered and the overall story had all been completed before Evangelion came out. I never meant Brain Powered to be an antithesis to Evangelion. I knew when I saw Evangelion that Brain Powered would be called an antithesis to it, but I didn't want to change my plans any, so I just resigned myself to that. This is connected with my wish for more animators to see themselves as entertainers. I don't think I succeeded with Brain Powered, and I don't think it was very good with entertainment--but there was one thing I did try to do with it. If 100 people come to see an anime with giant robots, then chances are are that not every one of those 100 people will be a huge fan of robot anime. What I wanted to do was to make an anime that had a truly interesting story that wouldn't cause the people who watched it to have a nervous breakdown. I also tried to make a story that would tell anime fans that there were often other things out there better than anime. That's the goal I challenged myself to do. I don't think the series itself was a success, though, I have to admit that. So I was very upset when I saw Evangelion, because it was apparent to me that the people who made it weren't thinking at all about making fun for or gaining the sympathy of the audience. Instead they tried to convince the audience to admit that everybody is sick, practically in the middle of a nervous breakdown, all the time. I don't think you should show things like that to everybody. It's not entertainment for the masses--it's much more interested in admitting that we're all depressed nervous wrecks, I thought. It was a work that told people it was okay to be depressed, and it accepted the psychological state that said if you don't like the way the world works, then it's okay to just pick up a gun and attack someone. I don't think that's a real work of art. When people see that, they begin to realize they are the same way. I think that we should try to show people how to live healthier, fuller lives, to foster their identity as a part of their community, and to encourage them to work happily until they die. I can't accept any work that doesn't say that. Animerica: Is that different from your downbeat endings? Tomino: I make sure my audience knows it's fiction and that what happens to my characters doesn't necessarily say anything about their own lives.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jun 11, 2008 11:23 pm honsou wrote: Its really a ideological difference, Tomino thinks that anime should be more about the world and society at large, while Anno wants anime that is more person centered.

[]:             Quote Post by honsou » Thu Jun 12, 2008 10:51 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Yeah, but that's ludicrous. The idea that art should merely be used as a teaching tool? You just end up spouting off variations on the Golden Rule. Life is more complex than that, and art is a reflection of that complexity.

[]:             Quote Post by Zerogoki » Thu Jun 12, 2008 6:59 pm honsou wrote: By positive i did not mean that the work has to be bright and sunny and nice. I meant it would have a positive effect on the audience. Your example of Grave of the Fireflies is a important one, because it shows how the horrors of war and quite literally kill children in the most horrifying ways possible. Ideally the movie would have the effect of giving the audience a true picture of what a children have to go though when they are in war torn countries and have a greater awareness of what war does to these children; therefore the audience would be less likely to support a war.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Thu Jun 12, 2008 7:33 pm Zerogoki wrote: So art must have a social conscience?
